nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, Pajama Cardinalfish gather in loose aggregations among branching coral colonies — particularly Porites — in sheltered bays and lagoons, then disperse toward the bottom at night to feed.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and prepared marine pellets/flakes. Tends to feed most actively at dusk and in dim light; multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised.

summary: The Pajama Cardinalfish (Sphaeramia nematoptera) is a peaceful, reef-safe schooling species widely considered one of the more beginner-friendly saltwater fish. Captive-bred specimens are broadly available and tend to adapt readily to aquarium life.
careNotes: Pajama Cardinalfish are slow, methodical swimmers that tend to hover near coral branches and rockwork during the day and become more active at dusk. They are generally kept in small groups of five or more, where they form loose hierarchies; isolated singles or pairs are also kept but the species' natural shoaling behavior is best expressed in a group. Provide ample branching rock, coral overhangs, or sheltered nooks for resting and hiding. Captive-bred individuals are broadly available and tend to adapt readily to prepared foods. Minimum tank size recommendations vary somewhat depending on group size and tankmates: around 76 L (20 gal) is commonly cited for a single specimen, roughly 114 L (30 gal) for a small school, and 120 L or more is generally advised when keeping a group of five alongside other species.
breedingNotes: The Pajama Cardinalfish is a paternal mouthbrooder: after courtship the female releases a clutch of roughly 200 or more eggs, which the male incubates orally for approximately 7 to 10 days until hatching. Brooding males typically refuse food during incubation and develop a visibly distended jaw. Spawning has been reported on cycles of approximately 2 to 3 weeks in well-maintained pairs. Fry are very small and require rotifers, transitioning to baby brine shrimp as they grow, for successful rearing. Captive-bred stock is broadly available in the trade, making it possible to start with already-acclimated fish rather than wild-caught specimens.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Apogonidae / Kurtiformes), max length 8.5 cm TL, Indo-Pacific range, depth 1-14 m, wild temperature 26.3-29.3 C, paternal mouthbrooding.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (20+ gal singly), temperament (peaceful/timid), reef-safe, diet, lifespan 2-5 yr, mouthbrooding cadence, and shoaling behavior (3-5 fish).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World - Pajama Cardinal care guide
additional.notes: Aggregated via WebSearch (direct fetch returned 404). Confirmed 30 gal minimum for school, temperature 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, reef-safe, mouthbrooding.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs form readily in captivity, and spawning itself is not the main challenge — the limiting step is rearing the very small fry, which require rotifers as a first food before transitioning to larger live foods.
